seo.title: Healthcare Code Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for code generation AI agents in healthcare. Covers HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, HITECH Act.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for code generation in healthcare environments introduces compounding risks: a single misconfigured agent can expose PHI, generate vulnerable medical software, or produce code that bypasses audit trails required by HIPAA and FDA 21 CFR Part 11. Unlike general software development, healthcare code generation must satisfy both software safety standards and patient data protection regulations simultaneously, where failures can result in patient harm, malpractice liability, and fines up to $1.5M per HIPAA violation. This checklist provides concrete, enforceable controls for every stage of the AI code generation lifecycle in clinical and health IT environments.

sections.heading: PHI Protection & Data Governance
sections.description: Ensure patient health information is never exposed to the LLM agent during code generation, satisfying HIPAA Safe Harbor and HITECH Act requirements for data minimization and breach prevention.

items.label: Enforce PHI De-identification Before LLM Prompt Injection
items.description: Implement an automated pre-processing pipeline that strips or replaces all 18 HIPAA Safe Harbor identifiers (names, dates, MRNs, IP addresses, etc.) from any code comments, variable names, sample data, or documentation before it reaches the LLM context window. Use NLP-based PHI detection tools such as AWS Comprehend Medical or Microsoft Presidio and log every redaction event.

items.label: Restrict LLM Processing to On-Premises or BAA-Covered Environments
items.description: Confirm that the LLM inference endpoint operates within your HIPAA-compliant boundary — either on-premises, in a private VPC, or via a cloud provider with a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA). Document the BAA reference number and renewal date in your compliance register.

items.label: Block Outbound Network Calls from the Code Generation Agent
items.description: Configure network egress rules so the LLM agent and its sandbox cannot make outbound HTTP/S calls to external APIs, package registries, or telemetry endpoints during code generation sessions. Use a network policy enforcer (e.g., Calico, AWS Security Groups) and alert on any blocked egress attempt.

items.label: Scan Generated Code for Hardcoded PHI Patterns
items.description: After each code generation iteration, run a static analysis pass using regex and ML-based PHI detectors to flag any generated code that contains patterns resembling SSNs, dates of birth, MRNs, or patient names. Reject and re-generate any code artifact that triggers a PHI match before it is written to disk.

items.label: Classify and Tag All Code Artifacts by Data Sensitivity
items.description: Apply automated data classification labels (e.g., PHI-Adjacent, De-identified, Public) to every generated code file, test fixture, and log output. Store classification metadata in a centralized catalog and enforce access controls based on label — PHI-Adjacent artifacts require role-based access with MFA.

items.label: Validate That Test Data Used in Sandbox Execution Is Synthetic
items.description: Before the agent executes generated code in the sandbox, verify that all input data files and environment variables are sourced from an approved synthetic data library (e.g., Synthea-generated datasets). Maintain a signed manifest of approved synthetic datasets and reject execution if any data source is not on the manifest.

items.label: Implement Data Residency Controls for Multi-Region Deployments
items.description: If the healthcare organization operates across multiple regions, enforce data residency policies that prevent code generation jobs, logs, and artifacts from being replicated to regions outside the approved jurisdiction. Configure cloud storage bucket policies and database replication rules to enforce geographic boundaries.

sections.heading: Code Safety & Secure Code Generation
sections.description: Validate that AI-generated code meets healthcare software safety standards, is free from security vulnerabilities, and cannot introduce attack vectors into clinical systems or medical devices.

items.label: Run SAST on Every Generated Code Artifact Before Execution
items.description: Integrate a Static Application Security Testing (SAST) tool (e.g., Semgrep, Checkmarx, Bandit for Python) into the agent's post-generation pipeline. Configure rules specific to healthcare risks: SQL injection in EHR queries, insecure deserialization of HL7/FHIR payloads, and hardcoded credentials. Block sandbox execution if any HIGH or CRITICAL severity finding is returned.

items.label: Enforce Credential and Secret Scanning on Generated Code
items.description: Run a secrets detection tool (e.g., Trufflehog, GitLeaks, detect-secrets) on every code artifact immediately after generation. Define custom patterns for healthcare-specific credentials: EHR API keys, FHIR OAuth tokens, DICOM server passwords, and database connection strings. Automatically redact detected secrets and alert the security team with the full generation context.

items.label: Validate Syntax and Compilation Before Sandbox Execution
items.description: Before executing any generated code, run a language-specific syntax validator and compiler check (e.g., `python -m py_compile`, `tsc --noEmit`, `javac` dry-run). Log the validation result with a timestamp and the agent session ID. Reject execution and trigger a re-generation cycle if syntax errors are detected, capping retries at 3 to prevent infinite loops.

items.label: Scan All Generated Dependency Declarations for Known Vulnerabilities
items.description: When the agent generates package manifests (requirements.txt, package.json, pom.xml), immediately scan declared dependencies against the NVD CVE database and OSS Index using tools like OWASP Dependency-Check or Snyk. Block installation of any dependency with a CVSS score ≥ 7.0 and require human approval for scores between 4.0–6.9.

items.label: Enforce Code Complexity and Size Limits Per Generation Iteration
items.description: Set hard limits on the size and cyclomatic complexity of code generated in a single iteration (e.g., max 500 lines, max cyclomatic complexity of 15 per function). Overly complex generated code is harder to audit and more likely to contain hidden logic. Reject artifacts exceeding limits and instruct the agent to decompose into smaller, reviewable modules.

items.label: Require Human Code Review for Any Generated Code Touching Clinical Logic
items.description: Classify generated code by its functional domain. Any code that implements clinical decision logic, drug dosage calculations, diagnostic algorithms, or interfaces with FDA-regulated medical devices must be flagged for mandatory human review by a qualified software engineer before deployment. Document the reviewer's identity, credentials, and sign-off timestamp.

items.label: Validate FHIR/HL7 Payload Handling Code Against Schema Specifications
items.description: When the agent generates code that parses or constructs FHIR R4/HL7 v2 messages, automatically validate the generated parsing logic against the official FHIR JSON Schema or HL7 message structure definitions. Use a FHIR validator library (e.g., HAPI FHIR Validator) to test generated code against sample conformant and non-conformant payloads.

items.label: Check Generated Code for Insecure Randomness in Security-Sensitive Contexts
items.description: Detect use of non-cryptographic random number generators (e.g., `Math.random()`, `random.random()`) in generated code that handles session tokens, encryption keys, or patient ID generation. Enforce use of cryptographically secure alternatives (`secrets` module, `crypto.randomBytes`) via SAST rules and reject code that violates this pattern.

sections.heading: Sandbox Execution & Runtime Isolation
sections.description: Ensure the code execution environment is fully isolated, resource-constrained, and incapable of accessing production healthcare data or external systems, preventing lateral movement and data exfiltration.

items.label: Execute All Generated Code in an Ephemeral, Network-Isolated Container
items.description: Run generated code exclusively in ephemeral containers (e.g., Docker with --network=none, gVisor, Firecracker microVMs) that are destroyed after each execution session. The container must have no access to host networking, host filesystem mounts, or inter-container communication. Verify isolation configuration with automated tests before each deployment of the sandbox infrastructure.

items.label: Apply Resource Quotas to Prevent Denial-of-Service from Generated Code
items.description: Set hard CPU (e.g., 0.5 vCPU), memory (e.g., 512MB), and execution time (e.g., 30-second timeout) limits on sandbox containers using cgroups or Kubernetes ResourceQuotas. Generated code that exceeds these limits is automatically terminated, logged, and flagged for review. This prevents runaway loops or memory exhaustion from impacting clinical systems.

items.label: Mount Only Read-Only, Synthetic Filesystems in the Sandbox
items.description: Configure sandbox containers with read-only root filesystems and mount only pre-approved synthetic data directories as read-only volumes. Explicitly deny write access to any path outside a designated ephemeral /tmp directory that is wiped on container exit. Validate filesystem mount configuration in CI/CD pipeline before sandbox deployment.

items.label: Capture and Analyze All Sandbox System Calls via Seccomp Profiles
items.description: Apply a restrictive seccomp profile to sandbox containers that allows only the minimum required syscalls for code execution. Block syscalls associated with privilege escalation (ptrace, setuid), network socket creation, and raw device access. Log all blocked syscall attempts and alert the security team on any pattern suggesting sandbox escape attempts.

items.label: Validate Test Execution Results Against Expected Outputs Before Acceptance
items.description: After sandbox execution, compare test outputs against a predefined expected results manifest using deterministic assertions. For healthcare logic, include boundary condition tests (e.g., zero dosage, maximum age, null patient ID). Reject code that passes fewer than 95% of defined test cases and require the agent to refine before re-execution.

items.label: Scan Sandbox Container Images for Vulnerabilities Before Each Use
items.description: Before spinning up a sandbox container, scan the base image using a container vulnerability scanner (e.g., Trivy, Grype, Aqua Security). Fail the execution pipeline if the image contains any CRITICAL CVEs or HIGH CVEs with available patches. Maintain a registry of approved, scanned base images with expiration dates.

items.label: Implement Sandbox Execution Logging with Tamper-Evident Storage
items.description: Capture all sandbox execution events — stdout, stderr, exit codes, resource usage, and syscall summaries — and write them to a tamper-evident log store (e.g., AWS CloudTrail with S3 Object Lock, immutable Azure Blob Storage) with WORM retention for a minimum of 6 years per HIPAA requirements. Each log entry must include the agent session ID, user identity, and UTC timestamp.

sections.heading: Audit Trails, Compliance & Governance
sections.description: Establish end-to-end auditability of every AI-assisted code generation decision to satisfy FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic records requirements, HIPAA audit controls, and SOC 2 Type II evidence collection.

items.label: Generate a Signed Audit Log Entry for Every Agent Code Generation Event
items.description: For every code generation request, capture and cryptographically sign an audit record containing: requesting user identity, timestamp (UTC), LLM model version and temperature settings, input prompt hash (not the raw prompt if it could contain PHI), output artifact hash, and disposition (accepted/rejected/refined). Store signed records in an append-only audit database and verify signatures on retrieval.

items.label: Maintain a Complete Chain of Custody for All Generated Code Artifacts
items.description: Track every generated code artifact from initial generation through review, testing, approval, and deployment using a code provenance system. Each artifact must have a unique ID, SHA-256 hash, generation timestamp, agent session ID, and list of all human reviewers with their approval timestamps. This chain of custody must be producible on demand for FDA audits and HIPAA compliance reviews.

items.label: Enforce Role-Based Access Control with Least Privilege for Agent Interactions
items.description: Define distinct RBAC roles for the AI agent system: Agent Operator (can trigger generation), Code Reviewer (can approve artifacts), Compliance Officer (read-only audit access), and System Administrator (infrastructure only). Enforce separation of duties so no single user can both trigger generation and approve the resulting artifact for production deployment. Review role assignments quarterly.

items.label: Conduct Automated Compliance Checks Against FDA 21 CFR Part 11 Requirements
items.description: Implement automated checks that verify each generated code artifact and its associated audit trail meets 21 CFR Part 11 requirements: electronic signatures are linked to their signatories, audit trails are computer-generated and not modifiable, and system access is controlled. Run these checks as a gate in the CI/CD pipeline and produce a compliance report for each release.

items.label: Perform Quarterly SOC 2 Type II Evidence Collection for AI Agent Controls
items.description: Map each control in this checklist to the relevant SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria (Security, Availability, Confidentiality). Collect automated evidence quarterly: access logs, sandbox execution records, SAST scan results, PHI de-identification logs, and dependency scan reports. Store evidence in a dedicated compliance vault accessible to auditors with read-only credentials.

items.label: Implement a Formal Incident Response Procedure for AI Code Generation Failures
items.description: Define and document a specific incident response runbook for AI code generation failures in healthcare contexts. The runbook must cover: PHI exposure via generated code (HIPAA breach notification within 60 days), generation of clinically unsafe code (immediate escalation to Chief Medical Officer and CISO), and sandbox escape attempts (immediate isolation and forensic investigation). Test the runbook with a tabletop exercise annually.

items.label: Require Documented Risk Assessment Before Deploying Generated Code to Production
items.description: Before any AI-generated code is deployed to a production healthcare environment, require completion of a formal risk assessment documenting: the clinical impact of a code failure, the data sensitivity of systems the code touches, the results of all security scans, and the identity of the approving clinical informaticist or physician if the code affects clinical workflows. Archive the risk assessment with the deployment record.

items.label: Track and Report LLM Model Version Changes and Their Impact on Generated Code
items.description: Maintain a model version registry that records every LLM model update, fine-tune, or configuration change used in the code generation agent. When a model version changes, require re-validation of a standardized test suite of healthcare code generation prompts to detect behavioral drift. Document the validation results and obtain sign-off from the compliance officer before activating the new model version.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: HIPAA Security Rule (45 CFR § 164.312) requires implementation of technical security measures to guard against unauthorized access to PHI transmitted over electronic communications networks — this applies to any PHI that could be inadvertently included in LLM prompts or generated code artifacts. Your AI code generation pipeline must demonstrate that PHI de-identification occurs before LLM processing and that all transmission of code artifacts uses TLS 1.2 or higher.

FDA 21 CFR Part 11 mandates that electronic records used in FDA-regulated activities (including software validation records for medical devices and clinical systems) must have audit trails that are computer-generated, time-stamped, and protected from modification. AI-generated code used in FDA-regulated software must be accompanied by 21 CFR Part 11-compliant electronic records documenting the generation, review, and approval process — the AI agent cannot be the sole signatory on these records.

The HITECH Act (42 U.S.C. § 17931) extends HIPAA breach notification requirements to Business Associates and increases penalty tiers based on culpability — willful neglect violations carry fines of $50,000–$1.5M per violation category per year. If your LLM provider processes PHI without a valid BAA, or if generated code causes a PHI breach, your organization faces direct HITECH liability. Ensure every vendor in your AI code generation stack has executed a BAA and undergone a HIPAA security risk assessment.

SOC 2 Type II requires that controls are not only designed correctly but are operating effectively over a minimum 6-month observation period. For AI code generation systems, your SOC 2 auditor will examine evidence of consistent PHI de-identification, access control enforcement, sandbox isolation, and audit log integrity across the entire observation period — not just at a point in time. Automate evidence collection from day one of deployment to avoid gaps that could result in a qualified SOC 2 opinion.

cta.heading: Automate Your Healthcare Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI code generation agents against HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, and SOC 2 Type II controls — getting real-time alerts, automated evidence collection, and audit-ready compliance reports without manual overhead.
